hero_summary: Suznal is a non-standard name that circulates in some teaching environments, and it appears to be used for Shawq Afza transposed to Rast (C), rather than as a classical name more established than Shawq Afza itself.
short_identity: A non-standard teaching label usually understood as Shawq Afza on Rast (C), and it should not be confused with Suznak.

quick_facts.karar: Rast (C), if it is understood as Shawq Afza transposed to C
quick_facts.ghammaz: Follows the logic of Shawq Afza after transposition of the tonic
quick_facts.lower_jins: Theoretically referred back to the structure of Shawq Afza after transposition to Rast (C)
quick_facts.upper_jins: The composite upper logic of Shawq Afza continues after the tonic is moved
quick_facts.character: A circulating teaching label more than a standardized classical name
quick_facts.common_start: If used in this sense, it is best understood as Shawq Afza transposed to Rast (C)

sayr.summary: Suznal is not among the best-known standardized classical names in the major Arabic and Turkish references, so it should not be handled here as a firmly independent maqam name like Suznak or Shawq Afza. A modern published source, however, explicitly mentions it as Suznal = Shawq Afza on C, that is, Shawq Afza transposed to Rast (C). Some practical discussions support that usage without raising it to the status of a standard classical name. The most faithful formulation, then, is that Suznal is a circulating label in some teaching environments for a maqam best understood as Shawq Afza on Rast (C), not as a more famous or more standard name than Shawq Afza itself.
resting_tones: If understood as Shawq Afza on C, the tonic center is Rast (C)
The points of emphasis follow the logic of Shawq Afza after transposition of the tonic
It should not be treated as an independently documented maqam to the same degree as Shawq Afza itself
motion_notes: If one wants to explain it modally, the safest course is to return it to Shawq Afza, then explain that the difference lies in the transposition to C
In the standard Turkish references, Shawq Afza is a composite maqam with a descending sayr and an original tonic on Ajam Ashiran
If that same structure is transposed to Rast (C) under the label Suznal, the general idea remains the same, only with the tonic moved to Rast
It should not be confused with Suznak, because Suznak is a different maqam of the Rast family
common_path: Understand the term first as a non-standard teaching label
Theoretically refer it back to Shawq Afza
Apply the concept of transposition to Rast (C)
Maintain a clear distinction between it and Suznak in explanation and listening
common_modulations: If Suznal is used as in some oral or teaching contexts, it is most likely Shawq Afza on C
If the intended meaning is the more standard classical name in the references, Shawq Afza is the better term to use, not Suznal
If there is confusion in naming, the intended maqam is probably not Suznak, because Suznak is an entirely different Rast-family maqam built on Rast with Hijaz on the ghammaz and a tonic on Rast (C)
In the classical references this name is usually not unfolded as a fully independent maqam, but referred back to Shawq Afza after the tonic is moved
